key_insight: A physics-informed neural network can learn transient elastodynamic wave propagation in a bimaterial solid while enforcing elasticity, interface, and boundary physics, enabling accurate surrogate predictions of reflection and transmission.
engineering_relevance: This offers a fast, physics-consistent alternative to repeated finite-element simulations for impact and high-rate solid mechanics problems such as Split Hopkinson Pressure Bar analysis.
method: PINN trained on axisymmetric linear elastodynamics with initial, boundary, and interface conditions embedded in the loss.
Validated and supplemented with high-fidelity ANSYS Explicit Dynamics finite-element data.
Used as a continuous surrogate to predict unseen time instants and modified material properties.
evidence_signal: The model matched finite-element results for axial/radial displacements, face-averaged responses, and dominant stress/strain evolution, with mesh-sensitivity studies supporting numerical robustness.
watch_item: Performance may depend on careful loss balancing and validation when generalizing to new material pairs or more complex geometries.
paper_title: A Physics-Informed Neural Network Framework for Elastodynamic Wave Propagation in Bimaterial Systems
